docs.title: Shard
docs.text:
Shard is a Django-native component framework. It gives you a familiar model for building UI — props, state, slots, scoped styles — without leaving the Django ecosystem or adding a JavaScript build step.

Shard is a framework, not a widget library. It does not ship pre-built components. Instead, it provides conventions and primitives so you can author your own components the way you would in React or Vue, but lighter and less opinionated.

docs.title: What you get
docs.text: Feature Description Props Typed inputs declared on Python component classes State Server-owned data updated via @action methods Slots Wrap and compose markup like children / Vue slots Scoped styles Co-located .css files, automatically prefixed per component HTMX Partial re-renders after server actions Alpine.js Optional client-side behavior inside templates

docs.title: Quick example
docs.text:
# myapp/components.py
from shard import Component, Prop

class Card(Component):
    title = Prop(str, default="")
    template_name = "components/card.html"

{# page template #}
{% load shard %}
{% shard_scripts %}

{% component Card title="Profile" %}
  <p>Anything here is passed through the default slot.</p>
{% endcomponent %}

docs.title: Why Shard?
docs.text:
Django templates are great for rendering pages, but they lack a first-class component model. Most teams reach for React or Vue SPAs, which introduces a separate build pipeline, API boundaries, and operational complexity.

Shard keeps you in Django:

  • Components are Python classes in your apps
  • Templates are standard Django templates
  • Styles are plain CSS files beside your templates
  • Interactivity uses bundled HTMX and Alpine.js
  • No webpack, no Vite, no separate frontend repo

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Installation
  • Quickstart
  • Core concepts
  • Examples

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: The repository includes a runnable example app in example/.

docs.title: Running the demo
docs.text:
pip install -e ".[dev]"
cd example
python manage.py migrate
python manage.py runserver

Open http://127.0.0.1:8000/

docs.title: Layout
docs.text:
Multi-slot page shell with header, default, and footer slots.

{% component Layout %}
  {% slot header %}<h1>Shard</h1>{% endslot %}
  ...
  {% slot footer %}<small>Footer</small>{% endslot %}
{% endcomponent %}

docs.title: Card (wrapper)
docs.text: Accepts arbitrary slot content with optional title prop and scoped .card styles.

docs.title: Button (wrapper)
docs.text: Wraps label text with variant prop (default, primary, ghost).

docs.title: Alert
docs.text: Demonstrates @computed for dynamic CSS classes.

docs.title: Counter
docs.text: Stateful component with @action, @computed, @emits, and shard_htmx.

docs.title: TodoList
docs.text: Combines server state (items, draft) with Alpine client state (focused) and ActionResult events.

docs.title: Example file map
docs.text: File Purpose example/components.py All component classes example/templates/home.html Demo page composition example/templates/components/*.html Component templates example/templates/components/*.css Scoped styles

docs.title: Try modifying
docs.text:
1. Add a new prop to Button and use it in the template
  2. Create a Panel wrapper with a sidebar named slot
  3. Add @emits to Counter.decrement and log the event in Alpine
  4. Run python manage.py shard_list to verify registration

docs.title: Components
docs.text: A Shard component is a Python class that ties together props, optional server state, a template, and optional scoped CSS.

docs.title: Defining a component
docs.text:
from shard import Component, Prop

class Alert(Component):
    message = Prop(str, required=True)
    level = Prop(str, default="info")
    template_name = "components/alert.html"

docs.title: Class attributes
docs.text: Attribute Purpose template_name Django template path (required) component_name Registry name (defaults to class name) scope Override scoped CSS key (defaults to component name) stylesheets Explicit list of CSS template paths styles Inline CSS string (alternative to .css file) scoped_styles Set False to disable auto-scoping

docs.title: Template context
docs.text:
Every component template receives:

Variable Type Description component Component The component instance props dict Resolved prop values state dict Current server state computed dict Values from @computed methods slots dict Rendered slot HTML by name shard_id str Unique instance identifier shard_scope str CSS scope key shard_actions dict Action name → URL mapping

docs.title: Prop access in Python
docs.text:
Props are descriptors. Access them on self inside component methods:

class Counter(Component):
    step = Prop(int, default=1)

    @action
    def increment(self, state):
        state["count"] += self.step  # self.step returns resolved prop
        return state

docs.title: Root element convention
docs.text:
Component templates that use Shard tags must load the library:

{% load shard %}
<div {% shard_root component %} class="alert">

This outputs id="shard-<instance_id>" and data-shard-scope="<scope>", which HTMX and scoped CSS rely on.

docs.title: Inline templates
docs.text:
For small components, use TemplateComponent:

from shard import TemplateComponent

class Badge(TemplateComponent):
    label = Prop(str, default="")
    inline_template = "<span class='badge'>{{ props.label }}</span>"

docs.title: Registration
docs.text:
Components in <app>/components.py are auto-registered. Verify with:

python manage.py shard_list

docs.title: Philosophy
docs.text: Shard occupies a specific niche: making it easy to build components in Django, not shipping a catalog of ready-made widgets.

docs.title: Framework, not library
docs.text:
A library gives you pre-built pieces to import. A framework gives you patterns to build your own pieces.

Shard provides:

  • A Component base class
  • A props system
  • State persistence and action dispatch
  • Slot composition
  • Scoped CSS
  • HTMX and Alpine.js integration

You provide:

  • Component classes
  • Templates
  • Styles
  • Business logic

docs.title: Lighter than React/Vue
docs.text:
Shard deliberately avoids:

  • Virtual DOM
  • Client-side routing
  • Build tooling
  • npm dependencies in your app
  • A separate API layer for UI state

Server state lives on the server. Client interactivity is opt-in via Alpine.js. Most updates flow through HTMX partial renders.

docs.title: Less opinionated than full SPA frameworks
docs.text:
Shard does not dictate:

  • CSS methodology (BEM, utility classes, plain CSS — your choice)
  • Page layout structure
  • Form handling strategy (use Django forms if you want)
  • Authentication patterns

The only conventions are where files go and how components connect.

docs.title: Django ecosystem only
docs.text:
Shard uses:

  • Django templates
  • Django cache
  • Django static files
  • Django URL routing
  • Django management commands

HTMX and Alpine.js are bundled as static assets — no CDN dependency, no package.json in your project.

docs.title: When to use Shard
docs.text:
Good fit:

  • Server-rendered Django apps that need reusable UI pieces
  • Teams who want component composition without a SPA
  • Projects that value simplicity over client-side richness
  • Gradual enhancement of existing Django templates

Less ideal:

  • Highly interactive dashboards with complex client state graphs
  • Real-time collaborative editing
  • Offline-first applications

For those cases, a dedicated frontend framework may serve you better. Shard is for teams who want to stay in Django.

docs.title: Props
docs.text: Props are typed inputs passed into a component, similar to React props or Vue props.

docs.title: Declaring props
docs.text:
from shard import Component, Prop

class Button(Component):
    variant = Prop(str, default="default")
    disabled = Prop(bool, default=False)
    button_type = Prop(str, default="button")
    template_name = "components/button.html"

docs.title: Passing props in templates
docs.text:
{% component Button variant="primary" button_type="submit" %}
  Save
{% endcomponent %}

docs.title: Prop options
docs.text:
Prop(str)                        # optional string, default None
Prop(int, default=0)             # optional with default
Prop(str, required=True)         # must be provided
Prop(str, label="Display name")  # used in error messages

docs.title: Supported types
docs.text: Type Coercion str str(value) int int(value) float float(value) bool "true", "1", "yes" → True list[T] JSON array or Python list

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Props are validated when a component is instantiated. Unknown props raise PropValidationError:

# PropValidationError: Unknown prop 'colour' for Button.
{% component Button colour="red" %}{% endcomponent %}

docs.title: Accessing props
docs.text:
In templates:

<button class="btn--{{ props.variant }}"></button>

In Python:

self.variant       # via descriptor
self.props         # full dict
self.props["variant"]

docs.title: Props vs state
docs.text:
Props State Set by Parent / page template get_initial_state() and @action Mutable by component No Yes Persisted across HTMX Yes (frozen) Yes (updated) Use for Configuration, labels, variants Counters, form drafts, lists

Props are inputs. State is internal data that changes over the component's lifetime.

docs.title: Slots
docs.text: Slots let components wrap child content — the equivalent of React's children or Vue's <slot>.

docs.title: Default slot
docs.text:
Content between {% component %} and {% endcomponent %} becomes the default slot:

{% component Card title="User profile" %}
  <p>This paragraph is passed into the card.</p>
{% endcomponent %}

{# components/card.html #}
<article {% shard_root component %} class="card">
  <h2>{{ props.title }}</h2>
  <div class="card__body">{{ slots.default|safe }}</div>
</article>

Use |safe because slot content is pre-rendered HTML.

docs.title: Named slots
docs.text:
For layout components with multiple insertion points:

{% component Layout %}
  {% slot header %}
    <h1>Dashboard</h1>
  {% endslot %}

  <p>Main content uses the default slot.</p>

  {% slot footer %}
    <small>© 2026</small>
  {% endslot %}
{% endcomponent %}

{# components/layout.html #}
<div {% shard_root component %} class="layout">
  <header>{{ slots.header|default:""|safe }}</header>
  <main>{{ slots.default|safe }}</main>
  <footer>{{ slots.footer|default:""|safe }}</footer>
</div>

docs.title: Nesting components in slots
docs.text:
Slots can contain other components:

{% component Card title="Counter" %}
  {% component Counter initial=3 %}{% endcomponent %}
{% endcomponent %}

Each nested component gets its own instance ID, state, and scoped styles.

docs.title: Slots are persisted
docs.text: Slot HTML is saved to cache with the component instance. HTMX re-renders include the original slot content automatically — actions don't need to know about slots.

docs.title: Wrapper components
docs.text:
Card, Button, Layout — structural components that accept arbitrary content:

class Button(Component):
    variant = Prop(str, default="default")
    template_name = "components/button.html"

docs.title: Leaf components
docs.text: Counter, TodoList — self-contained components with state and actions. Usually placed inside wrappers, not the other way around.

docs.title: Slot-less components
docs.text:
Components without {{ slots.* }} in their template simply ignore wrapped content. Use self-closing syntax:

{% component Counter initial=0 %}{% endcomponent %}

docs.title: Empty slots
docs.text:
Check before rendering:

{% if slots.header %}
<header>{{ slots.header|safe }}</header>
{% endif %}

Or use the default filter: {{ slots.header|default:"" }}.

docs.title: State
docs.text: Server state is a dictionary owned by a component instance, persisted in Django's cache between HTMX requests.

docs.title: Initial state
docs.text:
Override get_initial_state() to set defaults:

class Counter(Component):
    initial = Prop(int, default=0)

    def get_initial_state(self):
        return {"count": self.initial}

Props can seed initial state, but state itself is mutable.

docs.title: Reading state in templates
docs.text:
<p>Count: {{ state.count }}</p>

{% if state.items %}
<ul>
  {% for item in state.items %}
  <li>{{ item }}</li>
  {% endfor %}
</ul>
{% endif %}

docs.title: Updating state with actions
docs.text:
State changes happen in @action methods. Return the updated state dict:

@action
def increment(self, state):
    state["count"] += 1
    return state

Shard persists the returned dict and re-renders the component.

docs.title: State persistence
docs.text:
Each component instance has a unique shard_id. State is stored in cache:

shard:state:<instance_id> → { component_name, props, state, slots }

Configure timeout via SHARD_STATE_TIMEOUT (default 24 hours).

docs.title: State lifecycle
docs.text:
Mount → get_initial_state() → persist → render
  ↓
HTMX action → load state → @action → save state → render
  ↓
(cache expiry) → instance lost

Expired state returns HTTP 404 on the next action. Design components so this is acceptable, or increase the timeout.

docs.title: Client state (Alpine.js)
docs.text:
For UI-only state (toggles, focus, animations), use Alpine.js via get_client_state():

def get_client_state(self):
    return {"open": False}

<div {% shard_alpine component %}>
  <button @click="open = !open">Toggle</button>
  <div x-show="open">Panel</div>
</div>

Keep server state for data that must survive HTMX round-trips. Use client state for ephemeral UI.

docs.title: Slots in state
docs.text: Slot HTML is persisted alongside state. When HTMX re-renders a component, wrapped content is preserved. You don't need to re-pass slots on each action.

docs.title: Scoped styles
docs.text: Shard scopes component CSS automatically so class names don't leak globally.

docs.title: Convention: co-located CSS
docs.text:
Place a .css file beside your component template:

templates/components/
├── card.html
├── card.css
├── counter.html
└── counter.css

Shard discovers card.css when rendering card.html and injects scoped styles automatically.

docs.title: Writing styles
docs.text:
Write normal CSS with simple class names:

/* card.css */
.card {
  border: 1px solid #ccc;
  border-radius: 0.75rem;
  padding: 1rem;
}

.card__title {
  font-size: 1.125rem;
  font-weight: 600;
}

docs.title: How scoping works
docs.text:
At render time, Shard prefixes every selector:

/* You write */
.title {
  color: red;
}

/* Browser receives */
[data-shard-scope="card"] .title {
  color: red;
}

Your template root element gets the scope attribute via {% shard_root component %}:

<article data-shard-scope="card" id="shard-abc123" class="card"></article>

docs.title: Scope key
docs.text:
The scope key defaults to a slugified component name (Card → card). Override per class:

class Card(Component):
    scope = "ui-card"  # → data-shard-scope="ui-card"

docs.title: Disabling scoped styles
docs.text:
class Card(Component):
    scoped_styles = False

docs.title: Inline styles
docs.text:
class Badge(Component):
    styles = ".badge { padding: 0.25rem 0.5rem; }"

docs.title: Explicit stylesheets
docs.text:
class Card(Component):
    stylesheets = ["components/card.css", "components/card-theme.css"]

docs.title: Media queries
docs.text:
@media blocks are handled correctly — inner selectors are scoped:

@media (max-width: 600px) {
  .card {
    padding: 0.5rem;
  }
}

docs.title: What scoping does not do
docs.text:
• Shadow DOM isolation (elements can still inherit global font/color)
  • Automatic class name hashing (use BEM or prefixes for clarity)
  • CSS Modules-style imports

Scoped styles prevent selector collisions, not inheritance. Global page styles still apply.

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
1. Use BEM-style names (.card__title) for readability
  2. Put {% shard_root component %} on the outermost element
  3. Avoid styling bare elements (p, a) without a class — scope helps but class selectors are clearer
  4. Page-level layout CSS belongs in your base template, not component CSS

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.10+
  • Django 4.2+

docs.title: Install from source
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/kurtisrogers/Shard.git
cd Shard
pip install -e .

docs.title: Development install
docs.text:
Includes pytest for running the test suite:

pip install -e ".[dev]"

docs.title: Add to a Django project
docs.text:
# settings.py
INSTALLED_APPS = [
    # ...
    "shard",
    "myapp",  # your app with components.py
]

urlpatterns = [
    # ...
    path("shard/", include("shard.urls")),
]

docs.title: Optional context processor
docs.text:
Expose framework metadata in every template:

TEMPLATES = [{
    "OPTIONS": {
        "context_processors": [
            # ...
            "shard.context_processors.shard",
        ],
    },
}]

This adds a SHARD dict with version, url_namespace, and debug.

docs.title: Static files
docs.text:
Shard bundles HTMX and Alpine.js. Load them once per page:

{% load shard %}
{% shard_scripts %}

In production, run collectstatic as usual. Shard assets live under shard/static/shard/.

docs.title: Cache backend
docs.text:
Component state (props, slots, server state) is stored in Django's cache framework. Configure any cache backend:

CACHES = {
    "default": {
        "BACKEND": "django.core.cache.backends.redis.RedisCache",
        "LOCATION": "redis://127.0.0.1:6379/1",
    }
}

For development, LocMemCache works fine.

docs.title: Recommended layout
docs.text:
myproject/
├── myapp/
│   ├── components.py          # Component classes
│   ├── templates/
│   │   └── components/        # Component templates + CSS
│   │       ├── card.html
│   │       ├── card.css
│   │       ├── counter.html
│   │       └── counter.css
│   └── views.py
├── templates/
│   └── pages/                 # Full page templates
│       └── home.html
└── settings.py

docs.title: components.py convention
docs.text:
Each Django app can have a components.py module at the app root. Shard's autodiscover imports it at startup and registers every Component subclass defined there.

# myapp/components.py
from shard import Component, Prop

class Card(Component):
    title = Prop(str, default="")
    template_name = "components/card.html"

You can also register components manually:

from shard.registry import register
from .widgets import DatePicker

register(DatePicker, name="DatePicker")

docs.title: URL configuration
docs.text:
Shard needs a URL prefix for HTMX action endpoints:

# urls.py
from django.urls import include, path

urlpatterns = [
    path("shard/", include("shard.urls")),
]

Actions are served at /shard/action/<instance_id>/<action_name>/.

docs.title: Template directories
docs.text: Add your app's template directory to TEMPLATES["DIRS"] or use APP_DIRS = True (default). Component templates are regular Django templates — no special loader required.

docs.title: Settings reference
docs.text: Setting Default Description SHARD_STATE_TIMEOUT 86400 Seconds to keep component state in cache SHARD_URL_NAMESPACE "shard" URL namespace for action routes SHARD_AUTODISCOVER True Import <app>.components on startup

docs.title: Verify installation
docs.text:
python manage.py shard_list

Lists all registered components with their props and actions.

docs.title: Quickstart
docs.text: This walkthrough builds a stateful counter component from scratch.

docs.title: 1. Create a component class
docs.text:
# myapp/components.py
from shard import Component, Prop, action

class Counter(Component):
    initial = Prop(int, default=0)
    step = Prop(int, default=1)
    template_name = "components/counter.html"

    def get_initial_state(self):
        return {"count": self.initial}

    @action
    def increment(self, state):
        state["count"] += self.step
        return state

    @action
    def decrement(self, state):
        state["count"] -= self.step
        return state

Shard auto-discovers components from <app>.components when the app is in INSTALLED_APPS.

docs.title: 2. Create the template
docs.text:
{# templates/components/counter.html #}
<div {% shard_root component %} class="counter">
  <p>Count: {{ state.count }}</p>
  <button type="button" {% shard_htmx component "increment" %}>+</button>
  <button type="button" {% shard_htmx component "decrement" %}>-</button>
</div>

docs.title: 3. Add scoped styles
docs.text:
/* templates/components/counter.css */
.counter {
  display: flex;
  gap: 0.5rem;
  align-items: center;
}

Place counter.css beside counter.html. Shard discovers it automatically and scopes selectors to [data-shard-scope="counter"].

docs.title: 4. Use it in a page
docs.text:
{% load shard %}
{% shard_scripts %}

{% component Counter initial=5 step=2 %}{% endcomponent %}

docs.title: 5. Run the server
docs.text:
python manage.py runserver

Click the buttons. HTMX posts to Shard's action endpoint, the server runs your @action method, persists updated state, and returns a re-rendered HTML fragment.

docs.title: What happened?
docs.text:
1. {% component %} created a Counter instance with props initial=5 and step=2
  2. State was initialized via get_initial_state() → {"count": 5}
  3. Props, state, and slots were persisted to cache under a unique instance ID
  4. HTMX hx-post hit /shard/action/<id>/increment/
  5. Shard loaded state, called increment, saved new state, re-rendered the template

That's the full loop. Everything else in Shard builds on these primitives.

docs.title: Composition
docs.text: Shard components compose through slots and nesting.

docs.title: Wrapper + leaf pattern
docs.text:
{% component Card title="Tasks" %}
  {% component TodoList placeholder="Add a task" %}{% endcomponent %}
{% endcomponent %}
  • Wrapper (Card) — provides structure and scoped styles, accepts slots
  • Leaf (TodoList) — owns state and actions

docs.title: Layout components
docs.text:
Multi-slot layouts organize page structure:

{% component Layout %}
  {% slot header %}<h1>Settings</h1>{% endslot %}
  <form>...</form>
  {% slot footer %}<button>Save</button>{% endslot %}
{% endcomponent %}

docs.title: Rendering children from Python
docs.text:
Inside a component method or template:

# Python
html = self.render_child("Counter", props={"initial": 5})

{# Template #}
{% shard_child "Counter" initial=5 %}

docs.title: When to nest where
docs.text: Approach Best for Slots in page template Page-specific composition {% shard_child %} in component template Fixed child structure render_child() in Python Dynamic children based on props/state

docs.title: Avoiding deep nesting
docs.text:
Three levels is usually enough:

Layout → Card → Counter

Deeper nesting works but makes slot content harder to reason about. Consider flattening or using page templates for complex layouts.

docs.title: Shared styles
docs.text: Each component scopes its own CSS. Wrappers don't style slot content unless you use deep selectors (avoid this). Let leaf components own their appearance.

docs.title: Props drilling
docs.text:
Pass props explicitly at each level:

{% component Card title="Counter" %}
  {% component Counter initial=3 step=2 %}{% endcomponent %}
{% endcomponent %}

There's no automatic prop forwarding. This keeps data flow explicit and debuggable.

docs.title: Computed values
docs.text: Computed values are derived from props and state, exposed to templates without storing them in state.

docs.title: Defining computed values
docs.text:
from shard import Component, computed

class Counter(Component):
    @computed
    def label(self) -> str:
        return f"Count is {self.state['count']}"

docs.title: Using in templates
docs.text:
<p>{{ computed.label }}</p>

Computed values are recalculated on every render. They are not persisted.

docs.title: When to use computed
docs.text: Use computed Use state Formatted display strings Values that change via actions CSS class names from props Lists, counters, drafts Derived booleans Anything HTMX actions modify

docs.title: Example: dynamic CSS class
docs.text:
class Alert(Component):
    level = Prop(str, default="info")

    @computed
    def css_class(self) -> str:
        return f"alert alert--{self.level}"

<div class="{{ computed.css_class }}">{{ props.message }}</div>

docs.title: Access in computed methods
docs.text:
• self.props / self.prop_name — resolved props
  • self.state — current server state
  • self.slots — slot content (rarely needed in computed)

docs.title: Performance
docs.text: Computed methods run synchronously during render. Keep them cheap — no database queries or external API calls. For heavy derivation, compute in @action and store in state.

docs.title: Lifecycle hooks
docs.text: Components can hook into the action dispatch lifecycle.

docs.title: before_action
docs.text:
Called before the action handler runs:

def before_action(self, action_name: str, payload: dict) -> None:
    if action_name == "delete" and not self.props.allow_delete:
        raise PermissionError("Delete not allowed")

Use for:

  • Authorization checks
  • Logging / analytics
  • Input normalization

docs.title: after_action
docs.text:
Called after the handler returns and state is normalized:

def after_action(self, action_name: str, state: dict) -> None:
    if action_name == "save":
        logger.info("Saved item %s", state.get("id"))

Use for:

  • Side effects that shouldn't block the response
  • Cache invalidation
  • Audit trails

docs.title: Render lifecycle
docs.text:
Shard does not currently expose before_render / after_render hooks. Override render() on your component subclass if you need custom render behavior:

def render(self, request=None):
    html = super().render(request=request)
  # post-process html
    return html

docs.title: Instance lifecycle
docs.text:
__init__ → get_initial_state() → persist() → render()

On HTMX:

from_storage / cache load → dispatch_action() → persist() → render()

Component instances are not long-lived objects. Each request creates or reconstructs an instance from cache.

docs.title: Nested components
docs.text: Render child components from within a parent component's template or Python code.

docs.title: Template: shard_child
docs.text:
{# components/dashboard.html #}
<div {% shard_root component %} class="dashboard">
  <section>{% shard_child "Counter" initial=0 %}</section>
  <section>{% shard_child "TodoList" placeholder="Tasks..." %}</section>
</div>

Each child gets its own instance ID, state, and scoped styles.

docs.title: Python: render_child
docs.text:
class Dashboard(Component):
    show_counter = Prop(bool, default=True)

    def get_extra_widgets(self, request):
        parts = []
        if self.show_counter:
            parts.append(self.render_child("Counter", props={"initial": 0}, request=request))
        return parts

docs.title: Nesting in page templates
docs.text:
You don't need shard_child for page-level composition. Use slots:

{% component Card %}
  {% component Counter %}{% endcomponent %}
{% endcomponent %}

Use shard_child when the parent component template always includes specific children. Use slots when the parent accepts arbitrary content.

docs.title: State isolation
docs.text:
Child components have independent state. A parent's @action cannot directly modify a child's state. Options:

  1. Lift state to the parent and pass as props (if children are stateless renderers)
  2. Use HTMX events to coordinate (@emits / listeners)
  3. Use Django session or database for shared state

docs.title: Performance
docs.text: Each nested component adds a cache entry. For pages with many components, use a production cache backend (Redis) rather than LocMem.

docs.title: Testing
docs.text: Shard uses pytest and pytest-django for its test suite.

docs.title: Run tests
docs.text:
pip install -e ".[dev]"
python -m pytest tests/ -q

docs.title: With coverage
docs.text:
python -m pytest tests/ --cov=shard --cov-report=term-missing

Current coverage: ~92% of the shard package.

docs.title: Test layout
docs.text: File What it covers test_props.py Prop validation, coercion, descriptors test_scoping.py CSS scoping engine test_state.py Cache persistence for props/state/slots test_component.py Actions, computed, hooks, events, nesting test_registry.py Component registration test_htmx.py HTMX and Alpine attribute builders test_templatetags.py Django template tags test_views.py HTTP endpoints and error responses test_styles.py Scoped stylesheet loading test_integration.py Example app end-to-end flows

docs.title: Test settings
docs.text: Tests use tests/settings.py, which extends the example project settings and adds tests/templates/ to TEMPLATES["DIRS"].

docs.title: Test components
docs.text: Isolated components live in tests/support/components.py and are registered automatically via tests/conftest.py. They do not pollute the example application.

docs.title: CI
docs.text:
The ci.yml GitHub Actions workflow runs on every push and pull request:

Job What it checks pre-commit All hooks in .pre-commit-config.yaml lint ruff check and ruff format --check test pytest on Python 3.10, 3.11, 3.12 (≥90% coverage) docs mkdocs build --strict

docs.title: Pre-commit
docs.text:
Install hooks locally so checks run before each commit:

pip install -e ".[dev]"
pre-commit install

Run manually against all files:

pre-commit run --all-files

Hooks include trailing whitespace, YAML/TOML validation, Ruff (lint + format), and Prettier (Markdown/YAML).

docs.title: Actions
docs.text: Actions are server-side methods that update component state in response to HTMX requests.

docs.title: Defining actions
docs.text:
from shard import action

class Counter(Component):
    @action
    def increment(self, state):
        state["count"] += 1
        return state

docs.title: Action signature
docs.text:
@action
def my_action(self, state, field: str = ""):
    # self    — component instance (access props via self.prop_name)
    # state   — copy of current state dict
    # **kwargs — POST parameters from HTMX (excluding CSRF token)
    return state

Always return the updated state dict, or an ActionResult for advanced cases.

docs.title: Wiring in templates
docs.text:
Use the shard_htmx tag:

<button {% shard_htmx component "increment" %}>+</button>

Or manually:

<button
  hx-post="{{ shard_actions.increment }}"
  hx-target="#shard-{{ shard_id }}"
  hx-swap="outerHTML"
>
  +
</button>

docs.title: POST parameters
docs.text:
Form fields in the HTMX request are passed as keyword arguments:

<input name="text" />
<button {% shard_htmx component "add_item" %}>Add</button>

@action
def add_item(self, state, text: str = ""):
    if text.strip():
        state["items"].append(text.strip())
    return state

Pass extra values with the shard_htmx tag:

{% shard_htmx component "remove_item" index=forloop.counter0 %}

docs.title: ActionResult
docs.text:
Return events or redirects alongside state:

from shard import ActionResult, action

@action
def save(self, state):
    # ... save logic ...
    return ActionResult.with_events(
        state,
        **{"item:saved": {"id": state["id"]}}
    )

Or with a redirect:

return ActionResult(
    state=state,
    redirect="/success/",
)

docs.title: Lifecycle hooks
docs.text:
def before_action(self, action_name, payload):
    # runs before handler

def after_action(self, action_name, state):
    # runs after handler

docs.title: Error handling
docs.text:
• Unknown actions → HTTP 404
  • Expired state → HTTP 404
  • Non-HTMX requests → HTTP 400

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
Actions require:

  • POST method
  • HX-Request header
  • Valid CSRF token (Django's CSRF middleware)

Shard does not expose GET endpoints for state mutation.

docs.title: Alpine.js integration
docs.text: Alpine.js is bundled for client-side interactivity that doesn't need a server round-trip.

docs.title: When to use Alpine
docs.text: Use Alpine for Use HTMX/@action for Toggle visibility Persisting data Focus management Validation with server rules Animations Lists, counters, form state Client-only filters Anything that must survive refresh

docs.title: Seeding Alpine state
docs.text:
Override get_client_state() on your component:

class Dropdown(Component):
    def get_client_state(self):
        return {"open": False}

<div {% shard_alpine component %}>
  <button @click="open = !open">Menu</button>
  <div x-show="open" x-transition>{{ slots.default|safe }}</div>
</div>

docs.title: shard_alpine tag
docs.text:
<div {% shard_alpine component %}></div>

Outputs:

x-data='{"open": false}'

Pass extra keys:

<div {% shard_alpine component tab="settings" %}></div>

docs.title: Combining Alpine and HTMX
docs.text:
Alpine handles immediate UI feedback. HTMX handles persistence:

<div {% shard_alpine component %}>
  <form {% shard_htmx component "save" %} @submit.prevent="$el.requestSubmit()">
    <input
      name="draft"
      @focus="focused = true"
      @blur="focused = false"
    />
    <button type="submit" :disabled="!focused">Save</button>
  </form>
</div>

docs.title: Listening for Shard events
docs.text:
Action responses trigger HTMX events. Listen with Alpine:

<div {% shard_alpine component %} @todo:added.window="focused = false"></div>

Or in shard.js / your own scripts:

document.body.addEventListener("shard:action-complete", () => {
  // any action completed
});

docs.title: Loading order
docs.text:
{% shard_scripts %} loads:

  1. htmx.min.js (defer)
  2. alpine.min.js (defer)
  3. shard.js (defer)

All scripts use defer, so order is preserved and DOM is ready.

docs.title: No build step
docs.text: Alpine is included as a static file. You write directives directly in Django templates. No x-data compilation or component registration on the client.

docs.title: Events
docs.text: Shard supports HTMX custom events for decoupled communication between components and page-level scripts.

docs.title: Declaring events
docs.text:
Use @emits above @action:

from shard import action, emits

@emits("counter:changed")
@action
def increment(self, state):
    state["count"] += 1
    return state

After the action completes, HTMX fires counter:changed on the document.

docs.title: ActionResult events
docs.text:
For dynamic event payloads:

from shard import ActionResult, action

@action
def add_item(self, state, text: str = ""):
    value = text.strip()
    if value:
        state["items"].append(value)
        return ActionResult.with_events(
            state,
            **{"todo:added": {"item": value}}
        )
    return ActionResult(state=state)

docs.title: Built-in event
docs.text:
Every action also triggers shard:action-complete:

document.addEventListener("shard:action-complete", () => {
  console.log("A component action finished");
});

docs.title: Listening in Alpine
docs.text: <div @counter:changed.window="onCounterChange()"></div>

docs.title: Listening in vanilla JS
docs.text:
document.body.addEventListener("todo:added", (event) => {
  const detail = event.detail;
  // detail contains the payload from ActionResult
});

docs.title: Redirects
docs.text:
return ActionResult(state=state, redirect="/done/")

Sets the HX-Redirect response header. HTMX navigates the browser.

docs.title: Event naming conventions
docs.text:
Use namespaced events to avoid collisions:

  • counter:changed
  • todo:added
  • form:saved

Shard does not enforce a schema — choose conventions that fit your app.

docs.title: HTMX integration
docs.text: Shard is built around HTMX for server-driven UI updates. HTMX is bundled — no CDN or npm install needed.

docs.title: Loading HTMX
docs.text:
{% load shard %}
{% shard_scripts %}

This loads htmx.min.js, alpine.min.js, and a small shard.js glue file.

docs.title: The action loop
docs.text:
User click
  → HTMX POST /shard/action/<instance_id>/<action>/
  → Shard loads state from cache
  → @action method runs
  → State saved
  → Component re-rendered
  → HTML fragment returned
  → HTMX swaps into DOM

docs.title: shard_htmx tag
docs.text:
Generates common HTMX attributes:

<button {% shard_htmx component "increment" %}>+</button>

Outputs:

hx-post="/shard/action/abc123/increment/" hx-target="#shard-abc123"
hx-swap="outerHTML"

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
<button {% shard_htmx component "search" trigger="keyup changed delay:300ms" swap="innerHTML" %}>
Option Default Description swap outerHTML HTMX swap strategy target #shard-<id> Target selector trigger (none) HTMX trigger expression include (none) hx-include selector **vals (none) Extra POST values as hx-vals

docs.title: Swap strategies
Events
docs.text:
Every action response includes an HX-Trigger header:

{ "shard:action-complete": true, "counter:changed": true }

Listen in Alpine or vanilla JS:

document.body.addEventListener("counter:changed", (e) => {
  console.log("Counter updated");
});

Declare events on actions with @emits:

@emits("counter:changed")
@action
def increment(self, state):
    ...

docs.title: Redirects
docs.text: Return ActionResult(redirect="/somewhere/") to trigger HX-Redirect.

docs.title: CSRF
docs.text: HTMX includes the CSRF token automatically when using hx-post on forms or elements within a page that has {% csrf_token %} in a form, or when hx-headers is configured. Django's CSRF middleware validates all action requests.

docs.title: Debugging
docs.text:
In development, open browser DevTools → Network. Action requests show:

  • Request URL with instance ID and action name
  • POST body with form fields
  • Response HTML fragment
  • HX-Trigger response header

Use python manage.py shard_list to see registered actions per component.

docs.title: shard.Component
docs.text: Base class for all components.

docs.title: Class attributes
docs.text:
• template_name: str
  • component_name: str
  • scope: str
  • stylesheets: list[str] | None
  • styles: str
  • scoped_styles: bool

docs.title: Methods
docs.text: Method Description get_initial_state() Return initial state dict get_client_state() Return Alpine.js seed data get_context_data() Template context dict get_computed_data() Computed values dict render(request=None) Render component to HTML render_child(component, props=, slots=, request=) Render nested component dispatch_action(name, payload) Run action handler persist() Save to cache action_urls() Dict of action URLs before_action(name, payload) Lifecycle hook after_action(name, state) Lifecycle hook

docs.title: Properties
docs.text:
• props — resolved prop dict
  • state — server state dict
  • slots — slot content dict
  • shard_scope — CSS scope key
  • instance_id — unique instance ID
  • pending_events — events from last action
  • pending_redirect — redirect from last action

docs.title: shard.Prop
docs.text: Prop(type, default=None, required=False, label="")

docs.title: Decorators
docs.text:
@action          # mark method as HTMX action
@computed        # expose method return in template context
@emits("event")  # declare HTMX events after action

docs.title: shard.ActionResult
docs.text:
ActionResult(state=None, events={}, redirect=None)
ActionResult.with_events(state, **events)

docs.title: Render functions
docs.text:
from shard import mount, render_component

html = mount(Counter, props={"initial": 5})
html = render_component(Counter, props={"initial": 5}, slots={"default": "<p>Hi</p>"})

docs.title: Registry
docs.text:
from shard.registry import register, get_component, get_all_components

register(MyComponent, name="MyComponent")
cls = get_component("MyComponent")
all_components = get_all_components()

docs.title: Exceptions
docs.text:
• ShardError — base exception
  • ComponentNotFoundError
  • PropValidationError
  • ActionNotFoundError
  • StateNotFoundError

docs.title: shard_list
docs.text:
List all registered components:

python manage.py shard_list

Output:

NAME       CLASS
--------------------------
Alert      example.components.Alert
  props: message, level
  actions: —
Button     example.components.Button
  props: variant, button_type
  actions: —
Counter    example.components.Counter
  props: initial, step
  actions: increment, decrement

Useful for verifying autodiscover and debugging registration issues.

docs.title: Settings
docs.text: All Shard settings use the SHARD_ prefix in Django settings.

docs.title: SHARD_STATE_TIMEOUT
docs.text:
Default: 86400 (24 hours)

Seconds to keep component state (props, state, slots) in cache.

SHARD_STATE_TIMEOUT = 60 * 30  # 30 minutes

docs.title: SHARD_URL_NAMESPACE
docs.text:
Default: "shard"

URL namespace for action routes. Must match your include("shard.urls") configuration.

SHARD_URL_NAMESPACE = "shard"

docs.title: SHARD_AUTODISCOVER
docs.text:
Default: True

When True, Shard imports <app>.components for every installed app at startup.

SHARD_AUTODISCOVER = False  # manual registration only

docs.title: Context processor
docs.text:
Add shard.context_processors.shard to expose SHARD in templates:

SHARD = {
    "version": "0.2.0",
    "url_namespace": "shard",
    "debug": True,
}

docs.title: Cache configuration
docs.text:
Shard uses Django's default cache. For production:

CACHES = {
    "default": {
        "BACKEND": "django.core.cache.backends.redis.RedisCache",
        "LOCATION": os.environ.get("REDIS_URL", "redis://127.0.0.1:6379/1"),
    }
}

docs.title: Static files
docs.text:
STATIC_URL = "static/"

Run python manage.py collectstatic in production. Shard assets are under shard/static/shard/.

docs.title: Template tags
docs.text: Load with {% load shard %}.

docs.title: shard_scripts
docs.text:
Include HTMX, Alpine.js, and Shard glue scripts. Place once per page, typically in <head> or before </body>.

{% shard_scripts %}

docs.title: component / endcomponent
docs.text:
Render a component with optional slot content.

{% component Card title="Hello" %}
  <p>Slot content</p>
{% endcomponent %}

Self-closing (no slot content):

{% component Counter initial=0 %}{% endcomponent %}

docs.title: slot / endslot
docs.text:
Named slot inside a component block.

{% component Layout %}
  {% slot header %}<h1>Title</h1>{% endslot %}
  Main content
{% endcomponent %}

docs.title: shard_root
docs.text:
Root element attributes for a component instance.

<div {% shard_root component %}></div>

Outputs: id="shard-<id>" data-shard-scope="<scope>"

docs.title: shard_action
docs.text:
URL for a specific action.

<form action="{% shard_action component 'save' %}"></form>

Prefer shard_htmx for most cases.

docs.title: shard_htmx
docs.text:
Generate HTMX attributes for an action.

<button {% shard_htmx component "increment" %}>+</button>
<button {% shard_htmx component "search" trigger="keyup changed delay:300ms" swap="innerHTML" %}>
<button {% shard_htmx component "remove" index=forloop.counter0 %}>

docs.title: shard_alpine
docs.text:
Generate Alpine.js x-data from get_client_state().

<div {% shard_alpine component %}>
  <div {% shard_alpine component tab="home" %}></div>
</div>

docs.title: shard_child
docs.text:
Render a nested child component from within a component template.

{% shard_child "Counter" initial=5 step=1 %}

Uses the parent component from context when available.
